{"agent":"reviewer_1","payload":{"summary":"The paper presents a novel approach to unsupervised learning of causal structures and latent variables using a variational autoencoder (VAE) framework. The authors propose a model that combines causal discovery with representation learning by leveraging non-linear additive noise models (ANMs) and a structural equation model (SEM) as priors. They aim to recover both the causal structure and latent variables from unstructured data, such as images, without prior knowledge of interventions.","significance_and_novelty":"The paper addresses a significant problem in causal inference and representation learning by aiming to recover causal structures and latent variables from unstructured data. The novelty lies in integrating causal discovery with VAEs using ANMs and SEMs, which is a departure from traditional methods.","reasons_for_acceptance":["The integration of causal discovery with VAEs is a novel approach that could advance representation learning.","Theoretical contributions regarding the identifiability of the model are significant, as they address challenges in causal inference.","The use of non-linear ANMs and SEMs as priors in a VAE framework is innovative and potentially impactful."],"reasons_for_rejection":["The paper lacks detailed proofs for some of the theoretical claims, particularly regarding the identifiability of the model.","Assumptions regarding the non-linearity of functions and Gaussian noise need more rigorous justification.","The computational complexity of the proposed method is not thoroughly analyzed, which could impact its practical applicability."],"suggestions":["Provide detailed proofs for the theorems, especially regarding model identifiability, to strengthen the theoretical foundation.","Clarify assumptions about non-linearity and noise, and provide empirical validation if possible.","Include a section on computational complexity to assess the feasibility of the method for large-scale applications.","Consider comparing the proposed method with more baseline models to highlight its advantages and limitations."],"rating":6,"confidence":4},"input_message":"Analyze the paper and provide a structured review.","context_used":"Paper source: reg_237_unsupervised_learning_of_causa.pdf

Variational inference is a way of performing inference by solving an optimisation problem. A popular
instance is the Variational Auto-Encoder (V AE) (Kingma & Welling, 2014) which aims to extract
a useful latent representation of the data by encoding and decoding it back. Traditionally the V AE
prior is assumed to be an isotropic Gaussian distribution and the aim is to extract independent
latent variables such as in the β-V AE (Higgins et al., 2016b) and FactorV AE (Kim & Mnih, 2018).
There are works which use hierarchical priors such as iteratively conditioning each variable on its
preceding variable in the Ladder-V AE (Sønderby et al., 2016)

2 R ELATED WORK
Our work lies on the intersection of causality, variational inference, representation learning, and
high-dimensional unstructured input domains.
Causal inference deals with determining the causes and effects from data. Causal discovery methods
generally focus on recovering the causal graph responsible for generating the observed data, e.g.
Spirtes & Glymour (1991); Chickering (2003) (for an overview of methods in see Peters et al. (2017)).
However, these methods are largely applied to structured datasets such as medical (Brooks-Gunn
et al., 1992; Sachs et al., 2005; Louizos et al., 2017) or economics data LaLonde (1986) where the
observed variables are provided by domain specialists. In contrast, we focus on unstructured data
where the variables are not provided a priori.

Our work lies on the intersection of causality, variational inference, representation learning, and
high-dimensional unstructured input domains.
Causal inference deals with determining the causes and effects from data. Causal discovery methods
generally focus on recovering the causal graph responsible for generating the observed data, e.g.
Spirtes & Glymour (1991); Chickering (2003) (for an overview of methods in see Peters et al. (2017)).
However, these methods are largely applied to structured datasets such as medical (Brooks-Gunn
et al., 1992; Sachs et al., 2005; Louizos et al., 2017) or economics data LaLonde (1986) where the
observed variables are provided by domain specialists. In contrast, we focus on unstructured data

6 C ONCLUSIONS
In this work we proposed a general model that naturally extends the variational learning framework
to learn a non-linear Structural Equation Model (SEM) as the prior, which enables causal learning to
be performed on perceptual modalities such as images and video. To reconcile the non-linearity of
SEMs while using Gaussian variables we proposed a fully differentiable method that locally linearises
the SEM to obtain a locally-Gaussian distribution. Furthermore, we relaxed the search over causal
graphs as a joint continuous optimization over non-linear causal functions f. The proposed method
shows promise to scale to high-dimensional data and moderately complex SEMs, however future
work should explore more large-scale data such as long videos and other input modalities.

In this work we proposed a general model that naturally extends the variational learning framework
to learn a non-linear Structural Equation Model (SEM) as the prior, which enables causal learning to

meta_review.summary: The paper introduces a novel approach to unsupervised learning of causal structures and latent variables using a variational autoencoder (VAE) framework with non-linear additive noise models (ANMs) and structural equation models (SEMs) as priors. All three reviewers acknowledge the novelty and potential impact of the integration of causal discovery with VAEs, highlighting its significance in advancing representation learning and causal inference. The reviewers agree on the theoretical contributions, particularly regarding the identifiability of the model and the innovative use of locally-Gauss
key_points: The paper presents a novel integration of causal discovery with VAEs using ANMs and SEMs, which is a significant departure from traditional methods.
Theoretical contributions regarding model identifiability are recognized as significant, addressing challenges in causal inference.
The method shows improvement over traditional VAE models in disentangling causal relationships, as evidenced by experiments on static and dynamic datasets.
Concerns are raised about the lack of detailed proofs for theoretical claims, particularly regarding identifiability, and the need for more rigorous justification of assumptions.
The paper lacks detailed information on the reproducibility of experiments and discussions on limitations and potential failure cases.

response_payload.summary: The paper presents a novel approach to unsupervised learning of causal structures and latent variables using a variational autoencoder (VAE) framework. The authors propose a model that combines causal discovery with representation learning by leveraging non-linear additive noise models (ANMs) and a structural equation model (SEM) as priors. They aim to recover both the causal structure and latent variables from unstructured data, such as images, without prior knowledge of interventions.
response_payload.significance_and_novelty: The paper addresses a significant problem in causal inference and representation learning by aiming to recover causal structures and latent variables from unstructured data. The novelty lies in integrating causal discovery with VAEs using ANMs and SEMs, which is a departure from traditional methods.
reasons_for_acceptance: The integration of causal discovery with VAEs is a novel approach that could advance representation learning.
Theoretical contributions regarding the identifiability of the model are significant, as they address challenges in causal inference.
The use of non-linear ANMs and SEMs as priors in a VAE framework is innovative and potentially impactful.
reasons_for_rejection: The paper lacks detailed proofs for some of the theoretical claims, particularly regarding the identifiability of the model.
Assumptions regarding the non-linearity of functions and Gaussian noise need more rigorous justification.
The computational complexity of the proposed method is not thoroughly analyzed, which could impact its practical applicability.
suggestions: Provide detailed proofs for the theorems, especially regarding model identifiability, to strengthen the theoretical foundation.
Clarify assumptions about non-linearity and noise, and provide empirical validation if possible.
Include a section on computational complexity to assess the feasibility of the method for large-scale applications.
Consider comparing the proposed method with more baseline models to highlight its advantages and limitations.

retrieval.base_query:
ABSTRACT
Endowing deep neural networks with the ability to reason about cause and effect
would be an important step to make them more robust and interpretable. In this
work we propose a variational framework that allows deep networks to learn
latent variables and their causal relationships from unstructured data, with no
supervision, or labeled interventions. Starting from an abstract Structural Equation
Model (SEM), we show that maximizing its posterior probability yields a similar
construction to a Variational Auto-Encoder (V AE), but with a structured prior
coupled by non-linear equations. This prior represents an interpretable SEM with
learnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. arnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. Unfortunately,
computing KL-divergences with this non-linear prior is intractable. We show
how linearizing arbitrary SEMs via back-propagation produces local non-isotropic
Gaussian priors, for which the KL-divergences can be computed efficiently and
differentiably. We propose two versions, one for IID data (such as images) which
detects related causal variables within a sample, and one for non-IID data (such
as video) which detects variables that are also related over time. Our proposal is
complementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experi lementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experiment with
recovering causal models from images, and learning temporal relations based on
the Super Mario Bros videogame.

The method successfully recovers the underlying causal structures in both static and dynamic datasets, outperforming traditional VAE models in disentangling latent representations.

ABSTRACT
Endowing deep neural networks with the ability to reason about cause and effect
would be an important step to make them more robust and interpretable. In this
work we propose a variational framework that allows deep networks to learn
latent variables and their causal relationships from unstructured data, with no
supervision, or labeled interventions. Starting from an abstract Structural Equation
Model (SEM), we show that maximizing its posterior probability yields a similar
construction to a Variational Auto-Encoder (V AE), but with a structured prior
coupled by non-linear equations. This prior represents an interpretable SEM with
learnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. arnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. Unfortunately,
computing KL-divergences with this non-linear prior is intractable. We show
how linearizing arbitrary SEMs via back-propagation produces local non-isotropic
Gaussian priors, for which the KL-divergences can be computed efficiently and
differentiably. We propose two versions, one for IID data (such as images) which
detects related causal variables within a sample, and one for non-IID data (such
as video) which detects variables that are also related over time. Our proposal is
complementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experi lementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experiment with
recovering causal models from images, and learning temporal relations based on
the Super Mario Bros videogame.

6 C ONCLUSIONS
In this work we proposed a general model that naturally extends the variational learning framework
to learn a non-linear Structural Equation Model (SEM) as the prior, which enables causal learning to
be performed on perceptual modalities such as images and video. To reconcile the non-linearity of
SEMs while using Gaussian variables we proposed a fully differentiable method that locally linearises
the SEM to obtain a locally-Gaussian distribution. Furthermore, we relaxed the search over causal
graphs as a joint continuous optimization over non-linear causal functions f. The proposed method

response_payload.summary: The paper introduces a novel approach to causal learning by extending the variational learning framework to accommodate non-linear Structural Equation Models (SEMs) as priors. This method allows for causal learning in perceptual modalities like images and videos by utilizing a locally linearized SEM to achieve a locally-Gaussian distribution. The approach also involves a joint continuous optimization over non-linear causal functions, which is an innovative step towards handling high-dimensional data in causal inference.
response_payload.significance_and_novelty: The paper presents a significant advancement in the field of causal learning by addressing the challenge of non-linear relationships in high-dimensional unstructured data. By extending the variational framework to include non-linear SEMs, the authors tackle a complex problem that has not been fully,
reasons_for_acceptance: The paper proposes a novel method for causal learning that extends the variational framework to non-linear SEMs, which is a significant contribution to the field.
The approach allows for causal inference in high-dimensional data such as images and videos, which is a challenging and impactful area of research.
The method is innovative in its use of locally linearized SEMs to achieve a locally-Gaussian distribution, providing a new perspective on handling non-linear relationships in causal learning.
reasons_for_rejection: The paper lacks extensive empirical validation on large-scale datasets, which limits the demonstration of its scalability and practical applicability.
The presentation of the method could be improved for clarity, particularly in explaining the technical details of the linearization process and the optimization over non-linear causal functions.

retrieval.base_query:
ABSTRACT
Endowing deep neural networks with the ability to reason about cause and effect
would be an important step to make them more robust and interpretable. In this
work we propose a variational framework that allows deep networks to learn
latent variables and their causal relationships from unstructured data, with no
supervision, or labeled interventions. Starting from an abstract Structural Equation
Model (SEM), we show that maximizing its posterior probability yields a similar
construction to a Variational Auto-Encoder (V AE), but with a structured prior
coupled by non-linear equations. This prior represents an interpretable SEM with
learnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. arnable parameters (such as a physical model or dependence structure), which can
be fitted to data while simultaneously learning the latent variables. Unfortunately,
computing KL-divergences with this non-linear prior is intractable. We show
how linearizing arbitrary SEMs via back-propagation produces local non-isotropic
Gaussian priors, for which the KL-divergences can be computed efficiently and
differentiably. We propose two versions, one for IID data (such as images) which
detects related causal variables within a sample, and one for non-IID data (such
as video) which detects variables that are also related over time. Our proposal is
complementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experi lementary to causal discovery techniques, which assume given variables, and
instead discovers both variables and their causal relationships. We experiment with
recovering causal models from images, and learning temporal relations based on
the Super Mario Bros videogame.
